title: Energy intensity level of primary energy (MJ/$2021 PPP GDP)
definition: Energy intensity level of primary energy is the ratio between energy supply and gross domestic product measured at purchasing power parity. Energy intensity is an indication of how much energy is used to produce one unit of economic output. Lower ratio indicates that less energy is used to produce one unit of output.
unit: megajoules per 2017 PPP dollar of GDP

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata

editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Trinidad and Tobago, Zimbabwe, Liberia, while the lightest include Puerto Rico (US), Ireland, Cuba; that contrast is the fastest way to read the energy intensity pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Energy intensity separates heavy industry, climate, and efficiency patterns from simple electricity access maps.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Energy productivity can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Net energy imports lives in the same category, so it can feel plausible until the high and low countries are checked.
Natural resource rents overlaps with part of the visible pattern, but the full country spread does not match.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
regionalSignals: Energy maps often separate access, consumption, and production mix.
Look for hydro, fossil-fuel, and low-access signals.

reviewNotes: Batch 4 promotion: strong industry-and-efficiency signal that complements electricity mix maps.
Practice-only because the MJ-per-GDP unit is technical.
